Title: The Plastid Terminal Oxidase: Its Elusive Function Points to Multiple Contributions to Plastid Physiology

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Same gene as Arabidopsis PTOX; diiron quinol oxidase
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of IMMUTANS/PTOX causes variegated leaves and defective chloroplast development linked to reduced carotenoid/chlorophyll synthesis
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Developmentally regulated; critical early after seed coat breakage
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Present in developing plastids and roots; only a few percent of WT abundance sufficient for proper leaf development
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light aggravates variegation; low light suppresses phenotype; temperature also modulates phenotype
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Random mutagenesis mutant analysis; complementation with PTOX constructs; developmental and light-response phenotyping
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. IMMUTANS is the Arabidopsis PTOX gene. 2. Its loss causes the classic variegated immutans phenotype. 3. The phenotype strongly links PTOX-mediated plastoquinone oxidation to carotenoid-dependent plastid biogenesis.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Loss leads to severe plastid developmental defects; overexpression can elevate ROS in some systems
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Marker gene for plastid development and redox engineering studies; caution needed due to variegation and ROS effects
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This is a synonymous historical name for AtPTOX rather than an independent gene

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Provides electrons to the plastoquinone pool in chlororespiration and interacts functionally with PTOX; NDH defects alter plastid redox state and can affect strigolactone-related branching and carotenoid-associated processes
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Present in etioplasts and responsive together with PTOX under some stress conditions
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Darkness; de-etiolation; stress conditions; branching phenotypes
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoprotection and hormone biosynthesis support via plastid redox poise
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review evidence from mutant phenotypes and physiological analyses of chlororespiration
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NDH reduces plastoquinone in plant chlororespiration and works functionally opposite PTOX. 2. NDH deficiency can partially rescue ΔPTOX variegation and affects branching/strigolactone-related traits. 3. NDH and PTOX are often coordinately regulated under stress.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation changes plastid redox balance and development
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential companion target when manipulating PTOX/redox pathways, especially for carotenoid-derived developmental traits
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included as a core common gene because the review repeatedly links it causally to nutrient-metabolism-related PTOX functions without classifying it as a simple pathway enzyme for the nutrient itself

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduces plastoquinone in algal chlororespiration and is coordinately upregulated with PTOX under nitrogen deprivation, affecting plastid redox balance and indirectly carotenoid-related metabolism
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Increased under nitrogen starvation together with PTOX
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports plastid redox poise influencing pigment metabolism and stress acclimation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review evidence from physiological and mutant studies in algae
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NDA2 is the major algal plastoquinone reductase in chlororespiration. 2. Together with PTOX it shapes dark redox poise and stress responses. 3. Its coordinated induction with PTOX during nitrogen starvation supports a whole-pathway acclimation model.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential algal engineering target for balancing reductant flow during nutrient stress
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Not a direct carotenoid biosynthetic enzyme but important in nutrient-metabolism-related redox support

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Bypasses plastoquinone-dependent phytoene desaturation; expression in Arabidopsis increased PTOX activity and ROS sensitivity, revealing PTOX functions beyond simply supplying PDS electron acceptor
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous expression in Arabidopsis and ROS/growth phenotyping
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CRTI does not require PQ for phytoene and zeta-carotene oxidation. 2. Its expression in Arabidopsis unexpectedly increased PTOX activity and PTOX inhibitor-sensitive ROS production. 3. This supports additional PTOX functions beyond carotenoid desaturation support.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced growth rates and elevated ROS sensitivity in transgenic lines
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential bypass tool for carotenoid engineering, but interactions with plastid redox enzymes must be considered
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Used in the review as an experimental probe, not a native focal plant gene

Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Inactivation rescues the variegated phenotype of ΔPTOX Arabidopsis, linking cyclic electron flow control to carotenoid/plastid-development outcomes
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic interaction analysis in mutant combinations
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PGR5 contributes to cyclic electron flow. 2. Its inactivation suppresses ΔPTOX variegation. 3. This reveals strong coupling between electron-flow partitioning and carotenoid-dependent chloroplast development.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbing CEF may affect photoprotection and energy balance
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Suggests that combinatorial tuning of PTOX with electron-flow regulators could mitigate plastid defects
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Included because the review presents it as a causal modifier of PTOX-related nutrient phenotype

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Plastoquinol oxidation / chlororespiration; indirectly supports carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Chlororespiratory plastoquinol oxidation branch in plastids
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Terminal oxidase step; downstream plastoquinol oxidation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes plastoquinol and reduces oxygen to water via a nonelectrogenic plastid terminal oxidase reaction
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies oxidized PQ required by phytoene desaturase and influences carotenoid pathway flux; also affects strigolactone derived from carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Supports carotenoid biosynthesis and chloroplast biogenesis; loss causes variegation and reduced carotenoid accumulation; in some contexts participates in astaxanthin accumulation and stress-responsive electron dissipation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Often developmentally regulated; stress responsive in many species
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: High during early plastid biogenesis; induced by high light and also by chilling, salt, drought, nitrogen or phosphate starvation in some species; present in nongreen tissues such as roots
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: High light; low light; chilling/cold; salt stress; drought; nitrogen starvation; phosphate starvation; darkness; de-etiolation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Chloroplasts and chromoplasts; also nongreen plastids for carotenoid-derived strigolactone biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoprotection, plastid development, and precursor supply for strigolactone hormone biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point under plastid development and stress conditions; not established as universal rate-limiting enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Diiron quinol oxidase; alternative oxidase family-like 4-alpha-helical bundle with ExxEH motifs
Pathway_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Stromal side of thylakoid membranes, mainly nonappressed regions of plastids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review evidence from mutant analysis (immutans/ptox loss-of-function), heterologous expression, liposome reconstitution, site-directed mutagenesis, complementation, in vivo fluorescence assays, metabolite/pigment profiling
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PTOX is a plastid terminal oxidase that oxidizes PQH2 and reduces oxygen. 2. It is crucial for early chloroplast biogenesis and indirectly supports carotenoid biosynthesis by maintaining oxidized PQ for PDS activity. 3. Loss of PTOX causes Arabidopsis immutans variegation and reduced carotenoid accumulation in several plastid types. 4. PTOX abundance and function are environmentally responsive and may contribute to redox poise, chlororespiration, photoprotection, and carotenoid-derived hormone metabolism.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: PDS; ZDS; NDH; NDA2; AOX (functional substitute in heterologous complementation)
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression can enhance ROS production and reduce growth; safety-valve role is limited under saturating light
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target for engineering carotenoid-rich tissues, plastid development robustness, and stress acclimation, but constitutive overexpression may increase ROS and impair growth; deployment should consider coordinated antioxidant capacity
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review paper; PTOX is the main summarized core gene. The paper emphasizes multiple species-specific PTOX isoforms (e.g., PTOX1/PTOX2) and unresolved physiological functions beyond carotenoid biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Phylogenomic survey of >100 PTOX protein sequences from GenBank/Phytozome and other genome resources; no unified accession dataset reported

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes desaturation of phytoene to zeta-carotene while reducing plastoquinone
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms an early direct precursor in carotenoid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Its activity depends on oxidized PQ availability and is therefore supported by PTOX; contributes to carotenoid accumulation in chloroplasts and chromoplasts
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in tomato and pepper fruits with elevated carotenoid levels
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review evidence from mutant phenotypes, biochemical pathway assignment, and expression correlation in carotenoid-rich tissues
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PDS uses oxidized PQ as electron acceptor during phytoene desaturation. 2. PTOX indirectly supports PDS activity by reoxidizing PQH2. 3. PDS is upregulated together with PTOX and ZDS in carotenoid-accumulating fruits.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful carotenoid engineering target, especially in combination with genes maintaining plastoquinone redox balance
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Mentioned as a major pathway enzyme in the review’s carotenoid biosynthesis discussion, but not the principal paper focus

Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes desaturation of zeta-carotene in the carotenoid pathway with plastoquinone redox involvement
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms downstream carotenoid intermediates toward end-product carotenoids
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Associated with increased carotenoid accumulation in chromoplast-containing fruits together with PTOX and PDS
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Upregulated in tomato and pepper fruits displaying increased carotenoid levels
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Photoprotection and precursor supply for apocarotenoid hormones
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Review evidence from pathway biochemistry and fruit expression studies
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ZDS functions in early carotenoid desaturation. 2. Its expression rises with PTOX and PDS in carotenoid-rich tomato and pepper fruits. 3. The review links ZDS function to plastoquinone redox metabolism in plastids.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for carotenoid biofortification in chromoplast-rich tissues when coordinated with plastid redox genes
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Secondary summarized pathway gene, not the main focus of the paper
